content.text: Searching for the right code fragment across a large repository is one of the most frequent and consequential actions a coding agent takes.

are the specialized utilities — spanning plain-text search, indexed symbol lookup, syntax-tree pattern matching, and embedding-based semantic retrieval — that enable an agent to locate the exact lines, definitions, or structural patterns it needs without reading every file in the repository.
Four distinct search layers have emerged, each suited to a different kind of question.
(ripgrep, grep, git grep) scans files line by line for literal strings or regular-expression patterns — the right tool for finding an import path, an error message literal, or a configuration key.

(ctags, universal-ctags, Language Server Protocol) builds structured indexes of named entities — function definitions, class declarations, variable assignments — and supports jump-to-definition and find-all-references across files.
(comby, ast-grep, tree-sitter queries) parses source code into syntax trees and matches code patterns that preserve structure, enabling safe refactoring across syntactic variants.

(embedding models paired with a vector store or BM25 index) represents code chunks as dense vectors and retrieves by conceptual similarity, useful when the agent knows what a piece of code does but not its exact name.
These layers are not alternatives — they are complementary. A well-equipped coding agent carries all four as distinct tools and calls each when its strengths apply.

A typical production repository contains hundreds of thousands of lines of code spread across thousands of files. No LLM context window is large enough to hold all of it at once. Even if it were, flooding the context with irrelevant code degrades response quality because the model must attend over signal mixed with noise. Code search solves this by extracting only the fragments that bear on the task at hand, keeping context small, focused, and accurate.
Without effective search, an agent faces two failure modes. The first is

: the agent generates code that collides with an existing function it could not see, uses an import path that does not exist, or proposes an architecture that contradicts established patterns in the codebase. The second is
: the agent reads entire files or directories indiscriminately, burning tokens on boilerplate and pushing the relevant lines to the periphery of its attention.
Effective code search also enables the agent to

. After proposing a change, the agent can search for all call sites of a modified function, confirm the change is backward-compatible, and check whether test files cover the modified path — all in seconds, without human intervention.
Precise search returns tens of lines rather than thousands, keeping each LLM call focused and reducing cost per agentic step.

ripgrep searches gigabytes of source code in under a second using SIMD-optimized byte matching. Symbol indexes answer jump-to-definition queries in milliseconds. Agents with fast search execute more iterations within a given wall-clock budget.
Symbol-aware and AST-aware search returns structurally correct results — it cannot accidentally match the string "parse" inside a comment when the query asks for the function definition named

tree-sitter has grammars for more than 150 languages. ast-grep and comby support multi-language structural search. A single agent can work across a polyglot monorepo using the same search abstractions.

Search tools return structured results (file path, line number, matched text) that can be piped into subsequent agent steps: read the matched file at the given line, extract the surrounding function, then reason about it.
An agent can search for all references to a symbol before and after modifying it, giving concrete evidence that no call site was broken.

No single search layer dominates all query types. Text search is fast and zero-setup but returns false positives for identifier names that appear in comments, strings, and unrelated contexts. Symbol search requires an index that must be kept up to date as the codebase evolves; stale indexes produce incorrect jump-to-definition results. AST/structural search requires the file to parse cleanly — partial or syntactically invalid code (common in the middle of an edit) cannot be queried structurally.

Semantic search adds infrastructure complexity: an embedding model, a vector database, and a chunking pipeline that must be re-run whenever the codebase changes materially. The results are approximate by nature — the top-k returned chunks may not contain the function the agent actually needs, particularly for rare or domain-specific identifiers that appear far from semantically obvious query terms.
The agent must decide which search layer to invoke and how to phrase the query. A poorly formulated regex or an overly broad semantic query returns noisy results, wasting the subsequent read step.
can appear thousands of times across a large repo. Without result limits and post-processing, the agent consumes its context budget on irrelevant matches.
ctags and embedding indexes are snapshots. If the agent edits a file and immediately searches the symbol index, it may see stale results from before the edit.
Code search operates on static text and structure. It cannot find all code paths that execute under a particular condition, only code that textually or structurally matches a pattern.
Each search layer operates on a fundamentally different representation of the source code.
treats each file as a sequence of bytes. ripgrep reads files using memory-mapped I/O and applies the pattern using a DFA (deterministic finite automaton) compiled from the regex. It respects

and similar ignore files automatically, filtering out build artifacts and vendored dependencies. Matches are returned as (file, line_number, line_text) tuples, optionally with context lines above and below the match.

works by parsing source files into an abstract syntax tree and recording every named entity at the time of indexing. Universal-ctags visits each file and produces a tags file — a tab-delimited database of (symbol_name, file, line, kind, scope). LSP servers maintain a richer in-memory symbol table updated incrementally as files change. A query like "find the definition of
" is answered by looking up the symbol in the tags file and returning its file and line.

operates after parsing. ast-grep and comby compile a pattern that describes a code structure — for example, a function call with two arguments where the first is a string literal — into a tree query. The query engine walks each file's AST and reports nodes that match the structural shape, regardless of variable names or whitespace. This is the correct tool for safe large-scale refactoring: replacing all calls of the form
without touching comments or similar-looking code in unrelated contexts.

represents each code chunk (typically a function or class, delimited by AST boundaries) as a dense floating-point vector produced by a code embedding model. When the agent submits a natural-language query, the query is embedded into the same vector space and the k nearest code chunks are retrieved by cosine similarity or approximate nearest-neighbor search. This excels at questions like "where is the rate-limiting logic?" when the implementation might be spread across several functions with names that do not literally contain the words "rate" or "limit".

attrs.alt: Code Search Tools architecture: a Coding Agent sends queries through a Search Dispatcher which routes to four parallel layers — Text/Regex Search (ripgrep), Symbol Search (ctags/LSP), AST/Structural Search (comby/ast-grep), and Semantic Search (embeddings/BM25). All four layers feed a Result Aggregator that ranks, deduplicates, and trims results to fit the context window.

content.text: The diagram shows the four search layers exposed to a coding agent through a dispatcher that selects the appropriate layer based on query type. The
handles literal and pattern queries with no index overhead. The
resolves identifier definitions and references using a pre-built ctags or LSP index. The
matches code patterns that respect parse structure. The

retrieves by concept similarity using dense embeddings. The Result Aggregator deduplicates overlapping matches, ranks results by relevance, and trims the output to a token budget before returning it to the agent.
Choosing the right search layer at each step is as important as having all four available.
when the exact string is known: an import path (

), an error message that was reported in a bug, a configuration key, a hardcoded URL, or any literal that will appear verbatim in the source. Also use it for broad sweeps — "list every file that imports
" — before narrowing with a more precise tool.

when the agent needs to find the definition of a named entity (class, function, constant, type alias) or all its reference sites. This is the right first step when implementing a feature that extends an existing API: find the interface definition, read it, find all current implementors, and then generate the new implementation consistent with the pattern.

when planning or executing a systematic refactor that must touch every call site of a particular pattern — deprecating a function signature, wrapping every occurrence of a bare string format with a safer alternative, or auditing all usages of a known-insecure API. The structural guarantee ensures no syntactically different but semantically identical occurrences are missed.

as an entry point when the agent has no identifier to start from: it knows the behavior it is looking for but not the name. A question like "how does this codebase handle retries?" or "where is user input validated before being passed to the database?" benefits from semantic retrieval as the first pass, followed by symbol and text search to drill into the returned fragments.

use semantic search for exact-match lookups — it is slower and less precise than ripgrep for known identifiers. Do not rely on symbol indexes for files the agent has just edited within the same agent step, as the index may not have been updated.
A practical coding agent exposes each search layer as a callable tool with a defined input schema and a bounded output schema. The following steps cover setting up the four layers from scratch.

). Wrap it as a tool that accepts a pattern, an optional glob for file types, and an optional directory root. Cap results at 50 matches by default. Return (file, line_number, line_text) tuples. Use the
Install universal-ctags. Add a script that runs
in the repo root to generate a tags database. Expose two tools:
. The definition tool reads the tags file; the references tool falls back to ripgrep with a word-boundary regex around the symbol name.
). Expose a tool that accepts a language, a structural pattern, and an optional directory. Patterns use the
metavariable syntax to match any node at that position. Return (file, line, matched_text) tuples.

Choose a code embedding model (e.g., the text-embedding-3-small model from OpenAI, or a locally hosted code embedding model). Chunk the repository at function/class boundaries using tree-sitter. Embed each chunk and store vectors in a local FAISS index or a hosted vector database. Expose a
tool that embeds the query and returns the k nearest chunks with their file paths and line ranges.
Each tool should return results in a consistent format: a list of objects with
fields. The agent's tool-calling loop can deduplicate on (file, start_line) when multiple search layers return overlapping results.

# A simple ripgrep tool wrapper for a coding agent
# pip install anthropic  (ripgrep must be installed separately: https://github.com/BurntSushi/ripgrep)

import json
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path


def rg_search(
    pattern: str,
    *,
    root: str = ".",
    glob: str | None = None,
    context_lines: int = 2,
    max_results: int = 50,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Search for a regex pattern using ripgrep.

    Returns a list of match dicts:
        {"file": str, "line": int, "text": str}
    Capped at max_results to keep context window usage bounded.
    """
    cmd = [
        "rg",
        "--json",
        f"--context={context_lines}",
        "--no-heading",
        "--with-filename",
        "--line-number",
        pattern,
        root,
    ]
    if glob:
        cmd += ["--glob", glob]

    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=30)
    matches = []
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        obj = json.loads(line)
        if obj.get("type") == "match":
            data = obj["data"]
            matches.append({
                "file": data["path"]["text"],
                "line": data["line_number"],
                "text": data["lines"]["text"].rstrip("\n"),
            })
        if len(matches) >= max_results:
            break
    return matches


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Find all places a Python codebase uses 'requests.get'
    hits = rg_search(r"requests\.get\(", root="./src", glob="*.py")
    for h in hits:
        print(f"{h['file']}:{h['line']}  {h['text'].strip()}")

# Symbol search using universal-ctags
# Install: brew install universal-ctags  or  apt install universal-ctags

import json
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path


def build_tags(repo_root: str = ".") -> None:
    """Regenerate the ctags index for the repository."""
    subprocess.run(
        ["ctags", "-R", "--output-format=json", f"--output={repo_root}/.tags", repo_root],
        check=True,
    )


def find_definition(symbol: str, tags_file: str = ".tags") -> list[dict]:
    """
    Return definitions of a named symbol from the ctags index.
    Each entry: {"name": str, "file": str, "line": int, "kind": str}
    """
    results = []
    with open(tags_file) as f:
        for raw in f:
            entry = json.loads(raw)
            if entry.get("name") == symbol:
                results.append({
                    "name": entry["name"],
                    "file": entry["path"],
                    "line": entry.get("line", 0),
                    "kind": entry.get("kind", "unknown"),
                    "scope": entry.get("scope", ""),
                })
    return results


# Example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    build_tags("./src")
    defs = find_definition("UserRepository")
    for d in defs:
        print(f"{d['kind']} {d['name']} at {d['file']}:{d['line']} (scope: {d['scope']})")

# AST structural search using ast-grep
# Install: cargo install ast-grep  or  npm i -g @ast-grep/cli

import json
import subprocess


def ast_search(
    pattern: str,
    language: str,
    root: str = ".",
    max_results: int = 30,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Search for a structural code pattern using ast-grep.

    pattern: ast-grep pattern string, e.g. 'console.log($MSG)'
    language: 'python', 'javascript', 'typescript', 'rust', etc.
    Returns list of {"file": str, "line": int, "text": str, "vars": dict}
    """
    cmd = [
        "sg", "run",
        "--pattern", pattern,
        "--lang", language,
        "--json",
        root,
    ]
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=60)
    matches = []
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        item = json.loads(line)
        match = item.get("match", {})
        matches.append({
            "file": item.get("file", ""),
            "line": match.get("start", {}).get("line", 0),
            "text": match.get("text", ""),
            "vars": {
                k: v.get("text", "") for k, v in item.get("metaVariables", {}).items()
            },
        })
        if len(matches) >= max_results:
            break
    return matches


# Example: find all Python function calls with a bare string as the first argument
if __name__ == "__main__":
    hits = ast_search(
        pattern='$FUNC("$STR")',
        language="python",
        root="./src",
    )
    for h in hits:
        print(f"{h['file']}:{h['line']}  {h['text'].strip()}")
        print(f"  FUNC={h['vars'].get('FUNC')!r}  STR={h['vars'].get('STR')!r}")

Consider a coding agent tasked with implementing a new feature: adding a rate-limit header to all outbound HTTP responses in a Python web service. The agent does not know the codebase and must orient itself before writing any code.
Step 1 — entry point via semantic search: the agent queries

and retrieves three chunks — a base middleware class, a request logging decorator, and an existing CORS header injection function. These establish the structural pattern: response headers are set in the middleware layer via
Step 2 — symbol search: the agent calls
to locate the exact file and line number of the base class it must extend. It reads the definition and the existing subclass implementations to understand the interface.
Step 3 — text search to find all existing header mutations:
returns 8 matches across 4 files, confirming the pattern is consistent and identifying every place that already touches response headers.
Step 4 — structural search to audit for any out-of-pattern header setting:
catches two additional sites where headers are set via attribute syntax rather than subscript notation. Both must also receive the rate-limit header.

# Rate-limit middleware generated by the agent after the search sequence above

from typing import Callable
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import Response


RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW = 60       # seconds
RATE_LIMIT_MAX_REQUESTS = 100


class RateLimitHeaderMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    """
    Inject rate-limit headers into every outbound response.
    Matches the pattern established in cors.py and logging_middleware.py.
    """

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next: Callable) -> Response:
        response = await call_next(request)
        # Headers follow the IETF draft-ietf-httpapi-ratelimit-headers spec
        response.headers["RateLimit-Limit"] = str(RATE_LIMIT_MAX_REQUESTS)
        response.headers["RateLimit-Window"] = str(RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW)
        # Actual remaining/reset values would be populated from a rate-limit store
        response.headers["RateLimit-Remaining"] = "100"
        response.headers["RateLimit-Reset"] = "60"
        return response

The entire search sequence — semantic orientation, symbol lookup, text search, structural audit — completes in under five seconds. The agent has read only the 40 most relevant lines of a 50,000-line codebase and generated an accurate implementation that is consistent with existing patterns.
Using Text Search for Symbol Lookup

Searching for a function name with ripgrep returns every occurrence of the string — in comments, string literals, test mocks, variable names that happen to contain the identifier, and documentation. For "find the definition of
", a text search returns dozens of false positives. Symbol search returns exactly the

without a result cap returns thousands of lines. The agent's context fills with irrelevant imports before it can read the code it needs. Every search tool call must enforce a maximum result count — typically 20–50 lines for initial orientation, with follow-up targeted searches if more context is needed.
Reading Entire Files Instead of Targeted Lines
A common agent pattern is to search for a file, then read the entire file with

or a full-file read tool. For a 2,000-line file, this wastes 1,950 lines of context on code irrelevant to the task. The correct pattern is: search to find the line number, then read a bounded window around that line using a line-range read (e.g.,
filter returns matches in binary files, auto-generated files, lock files, test snapshots, and compiled output. Always pass a file-type or glob filter to text and structural search tools.
Treating Semantic Search Results as Ground Truth

Semantic search returns the k nearest chunks by vector similarity, not the k most correct chunks. An embedding model may rank a comment or a test fixture higher than the actual implementation. Always follow a semantic search result with a symbol or text search to verify the returned chunk is the authoritative source, not a copy, mock, or documentation reference.
Layer searches in order of precision.

Start with the cheapest, most precise layer: if the exact identifier is known, use symbol search first. Fall back to text search for patterns, and semantic search only when no identifier exists. This minimizes unnecessary API calls and context usage.
Enforce result caps at the tool level.
parameter defaulting to a conservative value (20–30). The agent should increase this only when an initial search confirms the query is specific enough that a larger result set is safe.
Return file paths with every result.
Every search result must include the file path and line number. The agent must be able to follow up with a bounded file read at the returned location without another search.
Use word-boundary anchors in regex search.
to restrict matches to the standalone identifier.
Cache symbol indexes between agent steps.
ctags and embedding indexes are expensive to rebuild from scratch. Build them once at session start, and refresh them incrementally after file edits using a targeted re-index (
Filter out generated and vendored files.
Add a search configuration that excludes node_modules, vendor, __pycache__, .git, and build output directories. ripgrep respects .gitignore by default; ensure the repo's .gitignore is complete.
Use context lines for comprehension, not discovery.

(context) flag returns lines before and after each match. Use context lines when reading code for comprehension, but parse only the matching line when building a list of file paths to read next — context lines add noise to downstream processing.

ripgrep is among the fastest file-search tools available. It uses Rust's regex engine, which compiles patterns to DFAs and applies them with SIMD instructions on modern CPUs. On a 10GB repository with 5 million lines of code, a simple literal search completes in under 2 seconds. A complex regex search may take 5–10 seconds but remains practical for interactive agent use.

Symbol indexes scale to very large codebases with linear index-build time and sub-millisecond query time. Universal-ctags processes approximately 100,000 lines per second; indexing a 5-million-line codebase takes roughly 50 seconds. After the initial build, incremental updates (re-tagging a single changed file) complete in under 100ms.

Re-embedding an entire large repository is expensive and slow (minutes to hours depending on size and model). Use AST-boundary chunking so that unchanged functions do not need to be re-embedded after unrelated edits. A file-hash cache that skips unchanged files reduces rebuild time proportionally to the fraction of code edited.

FAISS flat indices work well for repositories up to roughly 100,000 code chunks. Above that, use an approximate nearest-neighbor index (HNSW or IVF) to keep query latency under 100ms. Hosted vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) handle millions of vectors with sub-10ms p99 query latency.

ripgrep and ast-grep both dispatch across all CPU cores by default, saturating I/O and CPU on multi-core machines. ctags is single-threaded for index building but can be parallelized by partitioning the file tree across processes. Agent frameworks that support parallel tool calls can execute text, symbol, and semantic searches simultaneously, cutting total search latency by 2–4x.

The agent's result aggregation step (deduplication, ranking, trimming) should target a fixed token budget. A practical budget is 2,000 tokens of search results per query, ensuring search context never crowds out the code the agent is editing.
Exact string / regex search across files
Searching only tracked files in a git repo
Structural search and refactoring via patterns
Hosted vector search with filtering and metadata
Enterprise code search platform with API access
Returns AST context around regex matches

Text search is the right default starting point: zero setup, instant results, and adequate precision for exact strings. Symbol indexing adds structure-awareness with a one-time setup cost that pays off across thousands of agent steps. Semantic search provides the capability to start from a concept rather than a name, but at the cost of infrastructure, index maintenance, and approximate results. LLM over full files bypasses search entirely but is token-inefficient and fails on large files.
Code search tools operate on the local filesystem within the repository root. When exposing them as tools in an agent, the primary security concern is
: a maliciously or accidentally constructed search pattern containing
or an absolute path could cause the agent to search outside the intended repository scope. Always canonicalize the search root and reject patterns that resolve to a path outside the allowed tree.
ripgrep, ctags, and ast-grep are invoked as subprocesses. Set a
of the repository root, avoid passing
, and explicitly forbid patterns that contain shell metacharacters. Use
with a list of arguments, never a single shell command string.
A pathological regex (exponential backtracking) can cause ripgrep to hang on certain inputs, though ripgrep's regex engine is designed to avoid this. Still, always set a
on subprocess calls and impose a maximum result count to prevent runaway memory allocation.

If the codebase contains committed secrets (private keys, tokens in test fixtures), search results will expose them to the agent's context window and potentially to any logging or monitoring around the agent's actions. Before deploying a code-search-enabled agent, audit the repository for committed secrets and remove them.
The ctags file and the embedding index may be written to the repository root. Ensure these files are excluded from version control (add them to
) and that their permissions do not expose repository structure to unauthorized readers.

When the agent edits a file and then immediately searches for the symbol it just defined, the ctags index will return stale results. Design the agent loop to either re-index after writes or use text search for symbols that the agent has created in the current session, falling back to the index only for pre-existing code.
Effective code search is a prerequisite for coding agents operating on large repositories: without it, agents either miss critical context or waste tokens reading irrelevant code.

Four complementary search layers cover the full range of lookup patterns: text/regex (ripgrep) for exact strings, symbol (ctags/LSP) for identifier definitions and references, AST/structural (ast-grep, comby) for code-structure patterns, and semantic (embeddings) for concept-first queries.

Every search tool must enforce a result cap, return file paths and line numbers, and support bounded file reads as the natural follow-up action — results are only useful if the agent can efficiently navigate to and read the matched code.

Layer searches in order of precision: symbol search first when an identifier is known, text search for patterns, semantic search only when no identifier exists. This order minimizes latency and token cost per agent step.
Symbol and embedding indexes must be rebuilt or incrementally updated when the codebase changes; stale indexes produce incorrect results that the agent will act on without detecting the error.
Path traversal, subprocess sandboxing, result volume caps, and secret exposure are the primary security and reliability concerns when running code search tools inside an agent loop.

For very large codebases (millions of lines), ripgrep and ctags scale linearly and remain fast; semantic search requires a vector database and chunking pipeline maintained outside the agent's real-time loop.
